threats.title: Miscalculation of Epoch Boundaries Causes Systematic Reward Misallocation
threats.hypothesis_description: The loop logic in `update_market` calculates `nextEpoch` as `i + BLOCK_EPOCH` instead of aligning to the actual epoch boundary. When `lastRewardBlock` is mid-epoch, this applies the current epoch's reward rate to blocks that actually belong to subsequent epochs, systematically corrupting protocol emissions.
threats.investigation_rationale: This mathematical flaw directly breaks the core business logic of epoch-based reward distribution, allowing users to receive incorrect reward amounts. It is exploitable by strategically timing `update_market` calls.
preconditions: lastRewardBlock is not perfectly aligned with a BLOCK_EPOCH boundary

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: nextEpoch calculation fails to use floor division to find the true next epoch boundary.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance sets cantoPerBlock, but the contract misapplies these rates to the wrong blocks.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a market is updated and lastRewardBlock is mid-epoch.

operational_questions: How much CANTO is misallocated per epoch transition?
Can an attacker strategically time updates to maximize their own rewards?
validation_requirements: Write a PoC demonstrating the difference between expected and actual rewards when crossing an epoch boundary.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify an upcoming epoch transition where the reward rate increases significantly.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait until the new epoch has started, but ensure update_market has not been called.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call update_market. The loop will apply the higher reward rate of the new epoch to blocks that belonged to the old epoch.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires monitoring of epoch boundaries and reward rate changes.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None directly, but protocol emissions are corrupted.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic overpayment or underpayment of rewards, draining protocol funds.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to fix the loop logic.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of smart contract state and epoch timing.
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must execute transactions around epoch boundaries.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: update_market calls immediately following epoch transitions.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Discrepancies between expected emission rates and actual CANTO distributed.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users frequently calling update_market at specific times.

threats.title: Silent Downcast Truncation in accCantoPerShare Allows Permanent Destruction of Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `update_market` function explicitly downcasts the calculated reward per share to `uint128`. In Solidity 0.8, explicit downcasts do not revert on overflow; they silently truncate. If `marketSupply` is extremely low, the value exceeds `type(uint128).max`, truncating the accumulator and permanently destroying all unclaimed user rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fundamental monotonicity invariant of the reward accumulator. An attacker can intentionally trigger this to wipe out other users' rewards or it can happen naturally in low-liquidity markets.
preconditions: cantoReward is large enough that (cantoReward * 1e18) / marketSupply > type(uint128).max

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Explicit downcast to uint128 without checking if the intermediate uint256 value fits.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time marketSupply is extremely low and cantoReward is non-zero.

operational_questions: Can an attacker force marketSupply to 1 wei?
What happens to existing users' rewardDebt when accCantoPerShare truncates?
validation_requirements: Simulate a scenario with 1 wei marketSupply and verify the truncation of accCantoPerShare.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker waits for a period of low liquidity in a whitelisted market, or provides the only liquidity.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker withdraws almost all liquidity, leaving exactly 1 wei as the marketSupply.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker waits for a few blocks to accumulate cantoReward.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker calls update_market. The calculation exceeds uint128.max, causing silent truncation and resetting accCantoPerShare.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires the ability to manipulate the total supply of a lending market.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of unclaimed rewards for all users in the affected market.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade and manual state correction.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Reward claiming for the specific market.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of integer truncation and Solidity casting.
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires execution before other users deposit and increase marketSupply.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sudden drops in marketSupply followed by update_market calls.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Attacker performing large withdrawals followed by immediate updates.

threats.title: Market De-whitelisting Permanently Freezes Accrued User Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance de-whitelists a market, `update_market` reverts. Because `claim()` calls `update_market`, users are permanently blocked from claiming rewards they have already accrued. There is no grace period or emergency claim path.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a severe operational flaw where standard administrative action (deprecating a market) results in immediate, unrecoverable loss of user funds (rewards).
preconditions: Users have accrued but unclaimed rewards

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: update_market reverts if market is not whitelisted, and claim() depends on update_market.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately upon governance de-whitelisting a market.

operational_questions: Is there any alternative path for users to claim rewards if a market is de-whitelisted?
Does the lending market contract handle sync_ledger reverts gracefully?
validation_requirements: Test the claim function after a market is de-whitelisted.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance decides to deprecate a lending market and calls whiteListLendingMarket(market, false).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempt to call claim() to retrieve their accrued rewards.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transactions revert because update_market requires the market to be whitelisted.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users' rewards are permanently locked in the contract.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of user funds (rewards) and potential freezing of lending market withdrawals if sync_ledger reverts.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - governance must re-whitelist the market to allow claims.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Claiming and syncing for the de-whitelisted market.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after users have accrued rewards.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: whiteListLendingMarket calls setting status to false.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reverting claim transactions for specific markets.

threats.title: Delayed Market Updates Allow Retroactive Modification of Past Epoch Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: Because rewards are evaluated lazily, governance can call `setRewards` to change the `cantoPerBlock` for past epochs. If a market has not been updated recently, the new rate will be applied retroactively to blocks that have already passed, altering historical rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the immutability of historical rewards. Governance can intentionally or accidentally inflate or deflate rewards for periods that have already elapsed.
preconditions: A past epoch has elapsed but update_market has not been called for a specific market
Governance calls setRewards for that past epoch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setRewards modifies cantoPerBlock without forcing an update_market for all whitelisted markets.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a past epoch has not been fully processed by update_market.

operational_questions: Can governance intentionally delay updates to manipulate rewards?
How often are markets updated in practice?
validation_requirements: Simulate setting rewards for a past epoch and observe the effect on a stale market's update.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: A market goes without an update_market call for an entire epoch.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance calls setRewards to drastically reduce or increase the cantoPerBlock for that past epoch.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: A user calls update_market or claim().
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The rewards for the past epoch are calculated using the newly set rate, retroactively altering the emissions.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires governance action and a stale market.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unfair distribution of rewards; potential theft of yield.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - governance can reset the rate, but damage may already be done if claims occurred.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must occur before the stale market is updated.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden shifts in expected reward output for stale markets.

threats.title: Unbounded Epoch Loop Causes Permanent Denial of Service for Stale Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: The `update_market` function contains a `while` loop that iterates over missed epochs, making an external call to `gaugeController` on each iteration. If a market is inactive for many epochs, the gas cost of this loop will exceed the block gas limit, permanently bricking the market.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a classic unbounded loop vulnerability that occurs naturally over time for inactive markets, leading to a permanent Denial of Service with no recovery mechanism.
preconditions: Market has not been updated for many epochs
The number of epochs times the gas cost of gauge_relative_weight_write exceeds the block gas limit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: while (i < block.number) loop makes an external call to gaugeController each iteration.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When a market has been inactive for a large number of epochs.

operational_questions: How many epochs does it take to hit the block gas limit?
Is there any way to partially update a market?
validation_requirements: Calculate the gas cost per iteration and determine the maximum number of catch-up epochs.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: A whitelisted market is abandoned or sees no activity for a long period (e.g., months).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The difference between block.number and lastRewardBlock grows large.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: A user attempts to call claim() or sync_ledger().
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The transaction runs out of gas due to the unbounded loop and repeated external calls, permanently preventing any state updates for that market.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent Denial of Service for the affected market.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade as there is no partial update mechanism.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires a long period of inactivity.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverting transactions with Out of Gas errors when calling update_market.

threats.title: Missing Decimal Scaling in Secondary Rewards Corrupts Third-Party Integrations
threats.hypothesis_description: The `secRewardsPerShare` calculation contains a `TODO: Scaling` comment and hardcodes `1e18` scaling. If a whitelisted market uses a token with non-18 decimals (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals), the secondary rewards will be drastically miscalculated, corrupting third-party integrations.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an incomplete implementation that will automatically fail if governance whitelists standard non-18 decimal tokens, breaking the protocol's stated goal of supporting third-party secondary rewards.
deployment_assumptions: Whitelisted market uses a token with non-18 decimals (e.g., USDC)
threats.config_evidence: LendingLedger.sol:65 - TODO: Scaling comment indicates missing logic for non-18 decimal tokens.
preconditions: Whitelisted lending market uses a token with decimals other than 18

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TODO: Scaling comment in the code, with hardcoded 1e18 scaling.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance must whitelist a market with a non-18 decimal token.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a non-18 decimal market is active.

operational_questions: Are there any plans to whitelist markets using USDC or USDT?
How do third-party platforms consume secRewardDebt?
validation_requirements: Deploy a market with a 6-decimal token and observe the secRewardsPerShare calculation.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance whitelists a lending market that uses a 6-decimal token (e.g., USDC).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users deposit into the market. marketSupply is tracked in 6 decimals.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: update_market calculates secRewardsPerShare using 1e18 scaling, dividing by a much smaller marketSupply.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: secRewardsPerShare is artificially inflated by a factor of 10^12, causing third-party platforms to over-distribute secondary rewards.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires governance to whitelist a specific type of market.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Severe miscalculation of secondary rewards, potentially draining third-party reward pools.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires fixing the scaling logic and coordinating with third parties.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of token decimals and scaling.
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Continuous while the market is active.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Massive secondary reward claims on third-party platforms.

threats.title: Market Re-whitelisting Creates Accounting Discontinuity and Reward Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: When a market is re-whitelisted, `lastRewardBlock` is updated to the current block, but `accCantoPerShare` is not reset. This creates a gap in rewards without adjusting existing users' `rewardDebt`, leading to accounting inconsistencies when they eventually withdraw.
threats.investigation_rationale: This state discontinuity breaks the mathematical relationship between a user's balance, the global accumulator, and their reward debt, potentially leading to underflows or lost rewards.
preconditions: Users have existing non-zero balances in the market

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: whiteListLendingMarket updates lastRewardBlock but leaves accCantoPerShare unchanged.

operational_questions: What happens to a user's rewardDebt if they withdraw immediately after re-whitelisting?
Does the protocol intend to support re-whitelisting?
validation_requirements: Simulate de-whitelisting, waiting, re-whitelisting, and then a user withdrawal.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: A market is de-whitelisted. Users cannot withdraw or claim.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Time passes. The market is re-whitelisted. lastRewardBlock is set to current block.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: A user who deposited before de-whitelisting calls sync_ledger to withdraw.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Their rewardDebt is updated based on the current accCantoPerShare, but they missed out on rewards during the de-whitelisted period. The accounting becomes inconsistent.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Accounting corruption and potential loss of user funds.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Reward claiming and syncing for re-whitelisted markets.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: whiteListLendingMarket calls toggling a market from false to true.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reverting sync_ledger calls due to underflows after re-whitelisting.

threats.title: Unverified Market Sync Allows Compromised Lending Markets to Drain Protocol Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `sync_ledger` function implicitly trusts the `msg.sender` (the whitelisted market) to report accurate `_delta` values for any user. If a whitelisted market is compromised or malicious, it can fabricate massive deposits to drain the protocol's CANTO reserves.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a critical violation of trust boundaries. The protocol delegates all balance accounting to external contracts without any cross-verification, creating a single point of failure for the entire reward system.
preconditions: A whitelisted lending market is compromised or acts maliciously

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: sync_ledger accepts arbitrary _delta values from msg.sender (the market) without verification.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a whitelisted market is compromised.

operational_questions: What security guarantees do the lending markets provide?
Is there any cross-checking of total supply?
validation_requirements: Review the architecture of the integrated lending markets for potential vulnerabilities.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker finds a vulnerability in a whitelisted lending market (e.g., a reentrancy or logic bug).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker exploits the market to force it to call sync_ledger with a massive positive _delta for the attacker's address.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The LendingLedger records the massive balance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker calls claim() to drain the entire CANTO balance of the LendingLedger.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires finding and exploiting a vulnerability in an external contract.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete drain of protocol reward funds.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Massive sync_ledger calls with unusually large deltas.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: A single user claiming the majority of the protocol's CANTO.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Exploitation patterns on external lending markets.

threats.title: Unbacked Reward Emissions Lead to Protocol Insolvency and Locked Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol lacks a mechanism to ensure it holds enough CANTO to cover the reward rates set by governance. If total claimable rewards exceed the contract's balance, early claimers will drain the contract, and later claimers' transactions will revert, permanently locking their rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a structural insolvency risk. Because `claim()` attempts a native CANTO transfer without checking solvency, a funding shortfall results in a hard Denial of Service for users attempting to claim legitimately earned rewards.
deployment_assumptions: Governance sets reward rates that exceed the actual CANTO balance held by the contract
threats.config_evidence: LendingLedger.sol:112 - setRewards allows setting arbitrary rates without checking contract funding.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool success, ) = msg.sender.call{value: uint256(cantoToSend)}(""); require(success, "Failed to send CANTO");

preconditions: Total claimable rewards across all users exceed the contract's CANTO balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setRewards allows setting arbitrary rates, and claim() attempts to transfer CANTO without checking solvency.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance sets the rates and must fund the contract.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When total claimable rewards exceed the contract's CANTO balance.

operational_questions: Is there monitoring to ensure solvency?
validation_requirements: Compare the emission rate against the contract's balance over time.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance sets a high cantoPerBlock rate.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The total accumulated rewards exceed the CANTO actually held by the LendingLedger.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Early claimers successfully withdraw CANTO. Later claimers' transactions revert because the contract is empty, locking their rewards.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - occurs naturally if funding doesn't match emissions.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Denial of Service for reward claims; loss of user trust.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - governance must send more CANTO to the contract.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When the contract runs out of funds.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverting claim() transactions due to failed CANTO transfers.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users rushing to claim rewards before funds run out.
